content.text: WebSockets transform a FastAPI application from a request/response system into a persistent, bidirectional communication platform — enabling real-time features that HTTP's stateless model fundamentally cannot support efficiently.

A WebSocket is a persistent, full-duplex TCP connection established through an HTTP upgrade handshake. Once the connection is open, both the client and server can send messages to each other at any time without the overhead of repeated HTTP request/response cycles. In FastAPI, WebSocket endpoints are declared with the
object, which provides an async interface for accepting connections, sending and receiving messages, and handling disconnections.
FastAPI's WebSocket support is built on Starlette's ASGI WebSocket implementation, which runs natively on the same asyncio event loop as HTTP endpoints — no separate server or process is needed.

HTTP follows a strict request/response pattern: the client must initiate every exchange, and the server can only respond. This is sufficient for most CRUD APIs, but it breaks down for scenarios requiring:

(held-open HTTP connections) — solve the problem partially but with significant overhead: repeated TCP handshakes, HTTP headers on every exchange, and server-side connection queuing that doesn't map naturally to async patterns.
allow server-to-client streaming but are unidirectional. WebSockets provide full bidirectionality over a single persistent connection.
— one TCP handshake amortized over the entire session lifetime
— no HTTP header overhead on each message after the upgrade; typical message overhead is 2–14 bytes (WebSocket frame header)
— client and server send independently without waiting for the other
— runs in FastAPI's asyncio event loop without threads
— supports both UTF-8 text frames and raw binary frames in the same connection
— thousands of concurrent open connections are manageable on a single asyncio worker
— there is no concept of status codes, content negotiation, or standard error formats within the WebSocket protocol itself
— unlike HTTP, each connection holds server-side state, complicating horizontal scaling across multiple instances
) cannot be sent with the browser WebSocket API after the initial handshake; tokens must be passed as query parameters or subprotocols
— the server cannot reopen a closed connection; clients must implement reconnection logic with backoff
— load balancers and reverse proxies require explicit WebSocket support (nginx
headers; AWS ALB with sticky sessions)
— some enterprise networks block or terminate long-lived connections
A WebSocket connection begins as an HTTP/1.1 request with specific upgrade headers:

GET /ws/chat HTTP/1.1
Host: api.example.com
Upgrade: websocket
Connection: Upgrade
Sec-WebSocket-Key: dGhlIHNhbXBsZSBub25jZQ==
Sec-WebSocket-Version: 13
, and from that point the connection is a raw TCP stream carrying WebSocket frames — no more HTTP. FastAPI handles this handshake automatically when
After the upgrade, data is exchanged as WebSocket frames. Each frame has a small header (2–14 bytes) containing:
— whether this is the final fragment of a message
— client-to-server frames are masked (XOR) per the spec; server-to-client frames are unmasked
FastAPI's WebSocket object abstracts all framing — the application works with complete messages, not individual frames.

call suspends the coroutine until a message arrives, yielding the event loop to handle other connections in the meantime. This is why async WebSocket handling is efficient — thousands of open connections share the same thread via cooperative multitasking.

await websocket.accept(subprotocol=None, headers=None)
# Accept the connection. Must be called before send/receive.
# subprotocol: negotiate an application protocol (e.g., "json", "mqtt")
# headers: add custom response headers to the 101 response

await websocket.close(code=1000, reason="Normal closure")
# Close the connection from the server side.
# code: WebSocket close code (1000=normal, 1001=going away, 1008=policy violation)

text = await websocket.receive_text()
# Wait for a text frame, return as str

data = await websocket.receive_bytes()
# Wait for a binary frame, return as bytes

message = await websocket.receive_json()
# Wait for a text frame, parse as JSON, return as dict/list

raw = await websocket.receive()
# Low-level: returns a dict with "type", "text"/"bytes" keys
# type is "websocket.receive" or "websocket.disconnect"

await websocket.send_text("Hello")
# Send a UTF-8 text frame

await websocket.send_bytes(b"\x00\x01\x02")
# Send a binary frame

await websocket.send_json({"event": "update", "data": {...}})
# Serialize dict to JSON and send as text frame

websocket.client          # Address tuple (host, port)
websocket.url             # Full URL including query string
websocket.headers         # Request headers (from the HTTP upgrade)
websocket.query_params    # Query parameters from the upgrade URL
websocket.path_params     # Path parameters from the route pattern
websocket.cookies         # Cookies from the upgrade request
websocket.state           # Per-connection request.state scratchpad
is raised when the client closes the connection or the connection drops:

from fastapi import WebSocket, WebSocketDisconnect

@app.websocket("/ws/{client_id}")
async def websocket_endpoint(websocket: WebSocket, client_id: str):
    await websocket.accept()
    try:
        while True:
            data = await websocket.receive_text()
            await websocket.send_text(f"Received: {data}")
    except WebSocketDisconnect as exc:
        print(f"Client {client_id} disconnected with code {exc.code}")
        # Run cleanup: remove from connection registry, persist state, etc.
    finally:
        # Always runs — safe place for cleanup regardless of how loop exits
        await cleanup_connection(client_id)
block is the safest place for cleanup because it runs whether the loop exits via
WebSocket endpoints support the same path and query parameter mechanisms as HTTP endpoints:

from fastapi import WebSocket, Query
from typing import Optional

@app.websocket("/ws/{room_id}")
async def room_websocket(
    websocket: WebSocket,
    room_id: str,
    token: Optional[str] = Query(default=None),
    user_id: Optional[str] = Query(default=None),
):
    await websocket.accept()
    # room_id from path, token and user_id from query string
    # ws://api.example.com/ws/general?token=abc123&user_id=u42
    ...
API does not support custom HTTP headers after the initial connection, so
cannot be sent the same way as in HTTP requests. Three approaches handle this:
Approach 1: Token in Query Parameter (most common for browser clients)

from fastapi import WebSocket, Query, HTTPException
from jose import jwt, JWTError

async def get_ws_user(
    websocket: WebSocket,
    token: str = Query(...)
):
    try:
        payload = jwt.decode(token, SECRET_KEY, algorithms=["HS256"])
        user_id = payload.get("sub")
        if user_id is None:
            await websocket.close(code=1008, reason="Invalid token")
            return None
        return user_id
    except JWTError:
        await websocket.close(code=1008, reason="Invalid token")
        return None

@app.websocket("/ws/feed")
async def authenticated_feed(
    websocket: WebSocket,
    user_id: str = Depends(get_ws_user)
):
    if user_id is None:
        return
    await websocket.accept()
    ...
: tokens in query parameters appear in server access logs and browser history. Mitigate by using short-lived tokens (60–300 seconds) dedicated to WebSocket upgrade only.

@app.websocket("/ws/feed")
async def cookie_auth_feed(websocket: WebSocket):
    session_cookie = websocket.cookies.get("session_id")
    if not session_cookie:
        await websocket.close(code=1008, reason="Not authenticated")
        return

    user = await validate_session(session_cookie)
    if not user:
        await websocket.close(code=1008, reason="Invalid session")
        return

    await websocket.accept()
    ...

Cookies are included automatically in WebSocket upgrade requests (same-origin or with appropriate CORS/cookie settings), making this the cleanest approach for browser clients with an existing session.
The client connects, and the first message must be a JSON authentication payload:

import json

@app.websocket("/ws/stream")
async def first_message_auth(websocket: WebSocket):
    await websocket.accept()

    # Expect auth message within 10 seconds
    try:
        auth_raw = await asyncio.wait_for(websocket.receive_text(), timeout=10.0)
        auth = json.loads(auth_raw)
        user = await validate_token(auth.get("token"))
    except (asyncio.TimeoutError, json.JSONDecodeError, KeyError):
        await websocket.close(code=1008, reason="Authentication required")
        return

    if not user:
        await websocket.close(code=1008, reason="Invalid credentials")
        return

    # Now handle authenticated session
    await websocket.send_json({"type": "auth_success", "user_id": user.user_id})
    ...
This approach is used in protocols like Slack's RTM API. It is flexible but adds one round-trip before the session is active.
Connection Manager: Broadcasting to Multiple Clients
Managing multiple simultaneous WebSocket connections requires a registry that tracks active connections and supports broadcasting:
A common pattern is an endpoint where the server pushes data to the client based on external events (database changes, queue messages, scheduled updates) rather than in response to client messages:
When the server must both send updates and receive commands from the client simultaneously,

import asyncio
from fastapi import WebSocket, WebSocketDisconnect

@app.websocket("/ws/interactive/{stream_id}")
async def interactive_stream(websocket: WebSocket, stream_id: str):
    await websocket.accept()

    stop_event = asyncio.Event()

    async def send_updates():
        """Push data to client continuously."""
        try:
            while not stop_event.is_set():
                data = await fetch_next_update(stream_id)
                await websocket.send_json(data)
                await asyncio.sleep(0.1)
        except Exception:
            stop_event.set()

    async def receive_commands():
        """Handle commands from client."""
        try:
            while True:
                raw = await websocket.receive()
                if raw["type"] == "websocket.disconnect":
                    stop_event.set()
                    break
                command = raw.get("text")
                await process_command(stream_id, command)
        except Exception:
            stop_event.set()

    await asyncio.gather(send_updates(), receive_commands())
Rather than sending raw strings, production WebSocket APIs typically define a typed message protocol using Pydantic:
WebSocket connections can be silently dropped by NAT devices, firewalls, and load balancers that close idle TCP connections. Application-level ping/pong prevents this:

Note: the WebSocket protocol also defines native PING/PONG frames (opcodes 0x9/0xA). Many WebSocket servers (including Uvicorn) handle these at the protocol level automatically — application-level ping/pong is for additional control or for clients that don't respond to protocol-level pings.
Architecture: Scaling WebSockets Across Multiple Instances
The fundamental scaling challenge with WebSockets: a client connected to
cannot receive a message broadcast from
unless there is a shared communication channel.

Client A ──┐
Client B ──┤──► FastAPI Instance (in-memory ConnectionManager)
Client C ──┘
works when all clients connect to the same process.

Client A ──► FastAPI Instance 1 ──► Redis Pub/Sub ◄── FastAPI Instance 2 ◄── Client B
                                         │
                                    FastAPI Instance 3 ◄── Client C
Each instance subscribes to Redis channels and forwards published messages to its local connections:
where multiple users edit a document simultaneously and see each other's cursors and changes in real time:

from fastapi import FastAPI, WebSocket, WebSocketDisconnect, Query
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Dict, Set, Optional, Literal, Any
import asyncio, json, time, uuid

app = FastAPI()

class Operation(BaseModel):
    """Operational Transform operation."""
    type: Literal["insert", "delete", "cursor"]
    position: int
    content: Optional[str] = None
    length: Optional[int] = None
    user_id: str
    timestamp: float

class DocumentSession:
    def __init__(self, doc_id: str):
        self.doc_id = doc_id
        self.connections: Dict[str, WebSocket] = {}  # user_id → websocket
        self.cursors: Dict[str, int] = {}            # user_id → cursor position
        self.version: int = 0

    async def join(self, user_id: str, websocket: WebSocket):
        self.connections[user_id] = websocket
        # Send current document state to new joiner
        await websocket.send_json({
            "type": "init",
            "version": self.version,
            "users": list(self.connections.keys()),
            "cursors": self.cursors,
        })
        # Notify others
        await self.broadcast({
            "type": "user_joined",
            "user_id": user_id,
        }, exclude=user_id)

    async def leave(self, user_id: str):
        self.connections.pop(user_id, None)
        self.cursors.pop(user_id, None)
        await self.broadcast({"type": "user_left", "user_id": user_id})

    async def apply_operation(self, op: Operation):
        self.version += 1
        await self.broadcast({
            "type": "operation",
            "op": op.model_dump(),
            "version": self.version,
        }, exclude=op.user_id)

    async def update_cursor(self, user_id: str, position: int):
        self.cursors[user_id] = position
        await self.broadcast({
            "type": "cursor",
            "user_id": user_id,
            "position": position,
        }, exclude=user_id)

    async def broadcast(self, message: dict, exclude: str = None):
        dead = []
        for uid, ws in self.connections.items():
            if uid == exclude:
                continue
            try:
                await ws.send_json(message)
            except Exception:
                dead.append(uid)
        for uid in dead:
            await self.leave(uid)

sessions: Dict[str, DocumentSession] = {}

@app.websocket("/ws/doc/{doc_id}")
async def document_editor(
    websocket: WebSocket,
    doc_id: str,
    token: str = Query(...)
):
    # Validate token before accepting
    user_id = await validate_token_for_ws(token)
    if not user_id:
        await websocket.close(code=1008, reason="Unauthorized")
        return

    await websocket.accept()

    # Get or create document session
    if doc_id not in sessions:
        sessions[doc_id] = DocumentSession(doc_id)
    session = sessions[doc_id]

    await session.join(user_id, websocket)

    try:
        while True:
            raw = await websocket.receive_text()
            msg = json.loads(raw)
            msg_type = msg.get("type")

            if msg_type == "operation":
                op = Operation(**msg["op"], user_id=user_id, timestamp=time.time())
                await session.apply_operation(op)

            elif msg_type == "cursor":
                await session.update_cursor(user_id, msg["position"])

            elif msg_type == "ping":
                await websocket.send_json({"type": "pong"})

    except WebSocketDisconnect:
        await session.leave(user_id)
        if not session.connections:
            del sessions[doc_id]  # Clean up empty sessions

# BAD — unhandled exception crashes the coroutine with a traceback
@app.websocket("/ws")
async def ws(websocket: WebSocket):
    await websocket.accept()
    while True:
        data = await websocket.receive_text()  # Raises WebSocketDisconnect on disconnect
        await websocket.send_text(data)
Always wrap the receive loop in
2. Sending to a closed connection without exception handling
3. Blocking the event loop inside WebSocket handlers

# BAD — blocks all other WebSocket connections while processing
@app.websocket("/ws")
async def ws(websocket: WebSocket):
    await websocket.accept()
    while True:
        data = await websocket.receive_bytes()
        result = process_image(data)   # CPU-bound — freezes event loop
        await websocket.send_bytes(result)
4. Storing tokens in query parameters for long-lived sessions
Query-parameter tokens are logged by every server, proxy, and CDN in the chain. Use short-lived upgrade tokens (valid for 60 seconds, single-use) rather than reusing the main session token.
5. Not cleaning up disconnected clients from the registry

manager.active_connections.append(websocket)
# If disconnect handling doesn't call manager.disconnect(websocket),
# the list grows unboundedly with dead connections
In a single-process asyncio application, the
dict is not subject to thread race conditions — but two coroutines can interleave at
points. If two users connect to the same document simultaneously, both might attempt to create the session:
(atomic in CPython's GIL-protected dict) or check after each
or expiry on WebSocket upgrade tokens

Tokens passed as query parameters for WebSocket authentication must have a very short TTL (60–300 seconds). Without expiry, a leaked URL (from logs, browser history, shared links) permanently authenticates subsequent connections.
— sending before accepting raises a runtime error.
, and all cleanup (registry removal, resource release) in
with Pydantic models for both inbound and outbound messages — it makes the protocol explicit, self-documenting, and validates inputs.
(ping/pong JSON messages every 30 seconds) in addition to protocol-level keepalives — not all proxies and load balancers forward protocol-level PING frames.
for broadcasting across multiple FastAPI workers or instances — in-memory
for WebSocket authentication rather than passing long-lived session tokens in query parameters.
— prevent a single user from opening unbounded connections, which could exhaust server resources.
in broadcast loops — remove dead connections immediately and continue broadcasting to live ones.
for the first authentication message to prevent unauthenticated connections from holding slots indefinitely.
— the server cannot reopen a closed connection, so clients must reconnect with jitter to avoid thundering-herd reconnection storms.
An asyncio-based FastAPI application can maintain tens of thousands of concurrent WebSocket connections per worker process. The limiting factors are:
— each connection is a file descriptor. Default OS limits (1024) must be raised (
— each Python coroutine and associated buffers consume ~30–100KB. At 50KB average, 10,000 connections use ~500MB.
— the event loop processes one coroutine at a time. If any individual handler blocks (CPU work, synchronous I/O), all other connections stall.
WebSockets require sticky sessions or a shared pub/sub channel at the load balancer level:
(session affinity) — the load balancer routes all connections from a client to the same backend instance. Simple but uneven under reconnection storms.
— the preferred approach for production; each instance only manages its own connections and communicates through Redis. A Redis Cluster handles pub/sub at millions of messages per second.
A single Uvicorn worker can typically sustain:
~50,000 small JSON messages/second across all connections combined
Latency under 1ms for message delivery within the same process
Redis Pub/Sub adds ~1–5ms round-trip latency for cross-instance broadcasting
module, and consider binary protocols (MessagePack, Protocol Buffers) over raw JSON for large payloads.
Low-level WebSocket client for testing and service-to-service connections
WebSocket testing in pytest without a running server
Synchronous WebSocket testing using Starlette's test client
Async Redis client for Pub/Sub-based broadcasting
High-level Pub/Sub abstraction (Redis, Postgres NOTIFY, in-memory)
Inspiration for the Pub/Sub pattern; not used in FastAPI but conceptually relevant
Alternative protocol with built-in reconnection, rooms, namespaces; has Python server (
Simple, low-frequency updates; easiest to implement
Live feeds, notifications, log streaming; no client-to-server needed
Chat, collaboration, games, live bidirectional data
Emerging standard; low-latency gaming, video; limited browser support

are often the better choice when the data flow is only server-to-client — they work over HTTP/1.1, are automatically reconnecting in browsers, and do not require special proxy configuration. WebSockets are the correct choice when the client also needs to send data back at high frequency or with low latency.

WebSockets provide persistent, full-duplex TCP connections after a one-time HTTP upgrade handshake, enabling real-time bidirectional communication that HTTP's request/response model cannot efficiently support.
must always be caught and cleanup performed in
The browser WebSocket API cannot send custom HTTP headers after the initial upgrade — authenticate via cookies, query-parameter tokens (short-lived), or a first-message credential exchange.
patterns work within a single process; multi-instance deployments require Redis Pub/Sub or equivalent shared state to broadcast across workers.
Define a structured, typed message protocol (Pydantic models with a
discriminator field) for both inbound and outbound messages — it makes the API contractual, debuggable, and maintainable.
The event loop serves all WebSocket connections concurrently; any CPU-bound or blocking synchronous operation inside a handler stalls all other connections — offload to
Application-level heartbeat (ping/pong messages on a timer) is essential for detecting stale connections and preventing NAT/firewall-induced silent drops on long-idle connections.
WebSockets are the right choice for bidirectional, low-latency, high-frequency communication; Server-Sent Events are sufficient and simpler for server-to-client-only streaming scenarios.
